one_line_ask: Rebuild the client's two flat raster tee concepts (front chest + full back) into clean, production-ready screen-print separations: isolate each of the 3 approved brand spot colors and force it to its exact PANTONE hex as a solid flat plate, vectorize every color layer, and export per-color separation vectors plus a combined proof PDF.
brief: Little Lighthouse Fund, a children's charity, is running a fundraising T-shirt campaign and needs its two established concepts turned into production-ready screen-print artwork. The design lead has handed off two flat, high-contrast raster concept mockups exported from their design tool — a front-chest lockup (a stylized lighthouse-and-wave badge with the words LITTLE LIGHTHOUSE) and a full-back design (a larger lighthouse scene with a supporter tagline) — plus a one-page brand-guidelines PDF that pins the campaign palette to exactly three approved spot colors: Beacon Red (PANTONE 186 C, hex #C8102E), Harbor Navy (PANTONE 2758 C, hex #1B2A5B), and Sand Cream (PANTONE 7401 C, hex #F0E6C8). The printer's constraints are strict and non-negotiable: MAXIMUM 3 spot colors per shirt, NO gradients, NO halftones, all lettering already flattened into the raster (no live fonts needed), and each color must sit on its own clean, solid separation so it can be burned to a screen. The as-supplied concept files are muddy: the flat art has slight anti-aliased fringing at the edges, a busy off-white paper-texture background bleeding into the art, and colors that drift a few shades off the approved PA
